claims.normalized_claim: In Carpenter v. United States (2018) the Supreme Court held that the government's acquisition of historical cell-site location information (CSLI) is a search under the Fourth Amendment and generally requires a warrant.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Roberts, C.J.), holding
counterevidence: Four justices dissented (Kennedy, Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch).
allowed_wording: Getting your historical location records is a search; police generally need a warrant.
prohibited_wording: The government can never get your location.
All phone data now needs a warrant.

claims.normalized_claim: Carpenter was decided on June 22, 2018 by a 5-4 vote; the majority opinion was by Chief Justice John Roberts, and Justices Kennedy, Thomas, Alito and Gorsuch each filed a dissent.
allowed_wording: In a 5-4 decision in 2018, Chief Justice Roberts wrote for the Court; four justices dissented separately.
claims.notes: Date/vote/author/dissents. A stray AI-summary says 6-3; it is wrong (see SRC-0001 note).

claims.normalized_claim: The Court did not overrule the third-party doctrine; it declined to extend it to cell-site location information.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, third-party doctrine discussion
allowed_wording: The Court refused to extend the third-party doctrine to location data; it did not throw the doctrine out.
prohibited_wording: The Court abolished the third-party doctrine.

claims.normalized_claim: The third-party doctrine — that information voluntarily shared with a third party loses its reasonable expectation of privacy — comes from Smith v. Maryland (1979, dialed phone numbers) and United States v. Miller (1976, bank records).
evidence_locations: Opinion, discussion of Smith and Miller
allowed_wording: The doctrine says if you share it with a company, you lose the privacy in it; it comes from 1970s cases about dialed numbers and bank records.

claims.normalized_claim: The records were obtained without a warrant under the Stored Communications Act (18 U.S.C. 2703(d)), which requires only a court order on 'specific and articulable facts' — a standard lower than probable cause.
allowed_wording: Police used a federal law that needed far less than a warrant.

claims.normalized_claim: The government obtained about 127 days of Carpenter's cell-site location information, roughly 12,898 location points tracing his phone's movements.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, facts (127 days; ~12,898 points)
allowed_wording: 127 days of records — almost 13,000 location points.

claims.normalized_claim: The case arose from a series of armed robberies of RadioShack and cell-phone (T-Mobile) stores in the Detroit, Michigan area in 2010-2011; a confederate confessed to the FBI and provided phone numbers, including Timothy Carpenter's.
allowed_wording: A string of store robberies near Detroit; a confederate gave the FBI the suspects' phone numbers.

claims.normalized_claim: Timothy Carpenter was convicted and sentenced to 116 years in prison.
allowed_wording: He was convicted and sentenced to 116 years.
claims.notes: Sentence per DOJ. Describe neutrally; the Supreme Court ruling concerned the search, not guilt.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court reasoned that CSLI provides a comprehensive, detailed, and effortless chronicle of a person's movements over time and is generated without meaningful voluntary choice, so sharing it with a carrier does not strip away its Fourth Amendment privacy.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court, qualitative-difference reasoning
allowed_wording: Location data maps your whole life and you don't really choose to create it, so sharing it doesn't surrender your privacy.

claims.normalized_claim: The four dissenting justices warned that the majority's line was vague and that drawing such lines was a task for legislatures rather than courts.
allowed_wording: The dissenters said the line was blurry and that Congress, not the Court, should draw it.

findings: Durable content hashes for SRC-0001/0002/0003 not yet captured; recheck before publish. Note: ignore the stray Cornell LII AI-summary that misreports the vote as 6-3 (true vote 5-4).
